prompt: A breathtaking National Geographic aerial drone photograph of a vast, ancient dark-green forest stretching to the horizon. In the deep foreground, heavily shadowed pine trees create a strong natural vignette, forcing the viewer's eye to an uncharacteristically intricate and complex clearing in the center. The entire perimeter of this large clearing is shaped with an unnatural precision that subtly, but unmistakably, traces the stylized silhouette of a major global map projection (like a simplified, unified world map), which immediately anchors the location as 'global.' Inside this map-shaped clearing, there is no single dividing line. Instead, the textures are arranged in a complex, proportional mosaic woven across the entire global silhouette. This intricate pattern ensures that exactly one-third of the total map space (representing the 8% job displacement) consists of visceral, highly textured patches of shattered, dark decaying timber in heavy shadow, scattered evenly across every continent. Interwoven perfectly throughout, taking up exactly two-thirds of the total map space (representing the 14% growth), are explosive, lush patches of bright green saplings bathed in concentrated spotlights of golden sunlight, creating a global landscape of regeneration. A tiny, barely visible dirt logging road with a dormant machine touches the edge, anchoring the massive scale and hinting at human labor and industry. Hyper-detailed, cinematic lighting, no text, no charts.
dataset_name: World Economic Forum: The Future of Jobs Report 2025
insight: Like a global map where scattered tracts of fallen timber give way to a massive, sunlit super-bloom, the pain of job displacement is eclipsed by unprecedented growth—while structural changes will eliminate 92 million jobs, they will simultaneously create a vibrant landscape of 170 million opportunities.
